pi_items.source: export_result.sh line 50 and task description confirm this as the required report output path. Task.json description (line 5) specifies: 'Create a JSON report at /home/ga/Desktop/alarm_threshold_report.json with the following format: {"device": "Pulse Oximeter", "threshold_value": <integer_value_found>, "alarm_message": "<message_displayed>"}.'

export_result.sh line 41 and task description confirm this as the required screenshot output path. Task.json description (line 5) specifies: 'Take a screenshot of the Alarm List showing the active alarm and save it to /home/ga/Desktop/alarm_trigger_evidence.png'.

Web search and clinical literature confirm that SpO2 low alarm thresholds in medical devices range from 80-95%, with 90% being the most common standard default for adult patients. The expected minimum of 80 represents the lower bound of clinically realistic SpO2 alarm thresholds. Sources: Turner Medical pulse oximeter alarm guides, NIH/PMC clinical literature on alarm settings in ICUs (https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4891882/), and medical device standards showing typical alarm range of 90±10%.

Clinical literature and medical device standards confirm that SpO2 low alarm thresholds above 95% are not typical for general adult monitoring, but may be used in specific contexts. The expected maximum of 95% represents a realistic upper bound for SpO2 low alarm configurations. Sources: infusion_safety_interlock validated_pi mentions 'optimal target ranges typically 90-96%', and alarm_audit task.json specifies normal ICU SpO2 range as >= 92%. The 80-95% range encompasses clinical practice across different patient populations and monitoring contexts.

privileged_info_summary: The spo2_alarm_limit_validation task requires an agent to experimentally determine the default 'Low SpO2' alarm threshold in OpenICE's Simulated Pulse Oximeter by incrementally decreasing SpO2 values in the simulated device until the alarm triggers. OpenICE is a real, open-source medical device interoperability platform developed by MD PnP Program at Massachusetts General Hospital (https://github.com/mdpnp/mdpnp). The task generates synthetic data using OpenICE simulators, not real patient data. The expected SpO2 threshold range of 80-95% aligns with clinical standards: 90% is the most common default low SpO2 alarm threshold across medical devices for adult patients, supported by clinical literature showing typical alarm settings of 88-95% in ICUs. The task requires creating a Simulated Pulse Oximeter device adapter, launching an Alarm List clinical application, systematically decreasing SpO2 values, capturing evidence screenshots, and generating a JSON report documenting the discovered threshold value and alarm message.
